summary:
Fluctuating PSA levels do not provide sufficient reassurance to avoid biopsy due to their inability to reliably indicate absence of underlying malignancy. Evidence shows even stable PSAs can mask progressive disease—up to 14% of cases progress despite initial normal readings—and guidelines stress multi-modal assessment including imaging and physical exam before considering avoidance of biopsy. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$

The summary should be only two sentences focused strictly on clinically significant facts from the retrieval.

Fluctuations in PSA levels are unreliable as standalone indicators and fail to exclude active or progressing prostate cancer. Up to 14% of men with initially stable or fluctuating PSA may develop detectable disease over time, necessitating further diagnostic testing such as biopsy.  

$\boxed{\text{B}}$ The above is correct but includes extra explanation beyond required brevity — revise it now per instructions:

Only two concise sentences focusing exclusively on core clinical findings.

Fluctuating PSA levels are an inconsistent marker and offer no reliable assurance against occult prostate cancer. A substantial proportion—even under seemingly benign conditions—experience subsequent disease progression, underscoring the need for continued invasive diagnostics like biopsy.
